coredata.dc:description: The nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots (N, B-CDs), synthesized via a hydrothermal method, were specifically designed for the detection of mercury and subsequently encapsulated within a three-dimensional (3D) porous aerogel matrix. The resulting aerogels displayed an exceptional adsorptive capacity for heavy metals, achieving a maximum adsorption capacity of 319.49 mg/g for Hg (II) and 263.16 mg/g for Cd (II). Notably, the aerogel provided a rapid response to Hg (II) at extremely low concentrations of 3.5 μM within a mere 15 min. Furthermore, the study highlighted the aerogel's sustainability by assessing its potential for regeneration and subsequent resource utilization. Remarkably, after five cycles of regeneration, the aerogel maintained up to 75 % of its original adsorption capacity. Additionally, the Cd (II)-adsorbing adsorbent was repurposed through sulfurization treatment for the catalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB), achieving a high degradation efficiency of 95 %. This integrated approach not only provides an effective strategy for managing heavy metal contamination in wastewater but also contributes to the goal of resource recovery and sustainable material use.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comprehensive characterization of N, B-CDs: (a) TEM image with particle size distribution; (b) Lattice fringe image of N, B-CDs; (c) Wide scan of XPS spectrum for N, B-CDs; (d-g) High-resolution XPS spectra for C1s, N1s, O1s and B1s; (h) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of N, B-CDs (the inset picture is the photograph of CDs solutions under ultraviolet/solar radiation); (i) Fluorescence emission spectra of N, B-CDs with different excitation wavelengths from 290 to 410 nm (the inset picture is the fluorescence normalization for N, B-CDs).

(a) FTIR spectra of MCC, TOMCC, and TOMCC-NH; (b) SEM images of MCC and TOMCC-NH; (c) Elemental mapping of TOMCC-NH; (d) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of TOMCC-NH aerogels (The inset picture is BJH aperture distribution); (e) Swelling ratio of the material (the inset pictures are photographs of the sample under sunlight and 365 nm UV irradiation before and after swelling); (f) Hypothetical structure of TOMCC-NH.

(a) Fluorescence response of N, B-CDs solution to varying Hg (II) concentrations (the inset picture is the comparison of the sample under 365 nm excitation); (b) Linearity between I 0/I and Hg (II) over concentration of 0–3.3 μM; (c) N, B-CDs fluorescence response to various metal ions (the inset picture is the corresponding photograph); (d) N, B-CDs fluorescence monitoring: Long-term stability over two weeks (line 1); Fluorescence variation in 1–15 min post‑mercury exposure at 3.5 μM (line 2); Fluorescence response to different Hg concentrations within 15 min (line 3); (e) Fluorescence lifetime of N, B-CDs; (f) UV–vis absorption of N, B-CDs before and after Hg (II) addition.

(a) The adsorption capacity of TOMCC-NH for Hg (II) and Cd (II) under different pH; (b-c) The effect of contact time on the adsorption of Hg (II)/Cd (II) in single and binary system (the inset pictures are pseudo-second-order models); (d) The intraparticle diffusion model for the adsorption; (e-f) The adsorption isotherms of Hg (II)/Cd (II) in single system (the inset pictures are Langmuir adsorption models).

(a) Wide XPS scan of TOMCC-NH before and after Hg (II) and Cd (II) adsorption; (b-d) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (b), N1s (c), and O1s (d) of TOMCC-NH before and after Hg (II) adsorption; (e) Hypothetical adsorption mechanism of TOMCC-NH towards Hg (II); (f) Desorption and regeneration of TOMCC-NH towards Hg (II) and Cd (II); (g) Fluorescence spectra of initial N, B-CDs, N, B-CDs-Hg and N, B-CDs-Hg-EDTA-2Na (the inset picture is relevant fluorescence variation); (h) Photographs of the fluorescence recovery.

(a) XRD patterns of MCC and CdS@TOMCC-NH; (b-c) Elemental mapping and SEM image of CdS@TOMCC-NH; (d-e) High-resolution XPS spectra of Cd3d (d) and S2p (e); (f) UV absorption spectra of MB degradation (the inset picture is relevant photograph of the samples); (g) Degradation efficiency of MB; (h) Effect of scavengers on the degradation of MB; (i) Catalytic efficiency of CdS@TOMCC-NH for MB after 6 cycles.
The preparative route of N, B-CDs encapsulated aerogel adsorbents.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots (N, B-CDs), synthesized via a hydrothermal method, were specifically designed for the detection of mercury and subsequently encapsulated within a three-dimensional (3D) porous aerogel matrix. The resulting aerogels displayed an exceptional adsorptive capacity for heavy metals, achieving a maximum adsorption capacity of 319.49 mg/g for Hg (II) and 263.16 mg/g for Cd (II). Notably, the aerogel provided a rapid response to Hg (II) at extremely low concentrations of 3.5 μM within a mere 15 min. Furthermore, the study highlighted the aerogel's sustainability by assessing its potential for regeneration and subsequent resource utilization. Remarkably, after five cycles of regeneration, the aerogel maintained up to 75 % of its original adsorption capacity. Additionally, the Cd (II)-adsorbing adsorbent was repurposed through sulfurization treatment for the catalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB), achieving a high degradation efficiency of 95 %. This integrated approach not only provides an effective strategy for managing heavy metal contamination in wastewater but also contributes to the goal of resource recovery and sustainable material use.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The ubiquitous issue of heavy metal ion pollution, particularly mercury contamination in aquatic ecosystems poses a significant and enduring threat to both human health and the environment [1,2]. The high toxicity of mercury is a major concern as it bioaccumulates in the food chain, causing serious health issues such as kidney damage, gastrointestinal and immune system disorders, and neurological impairments under chronic exposure [3,4]. To cope with this pressing environmental challenge, there is an imperative need for the development of effective mercury remediation strategies in aquatic systems. Among various water treatment techniques, adsorption is a practical and economical choice for removing heavy metals from water [5,6]. The key of adsorption technology is the development of novel adsorbents that can enhance the removal efficiency and reduce the associated costs [7].

Cellulose, the most abundant natural polysaccharide on earth, is an attractive material for adsorbents due to its widespread availability, low cost, and non-toxicity, which aligns with green chemistry principles [8,9]. However, the common powdered form of cellulose-based adsorbents poses challenges in recovery and reuse, which are essential for practical application and sustainability [10]. By utilizing cellulose's abundant hydroxyl groups, crosslinking can be used to create a molding material with porous structure and expanded specific surface area, culminating in the formation of aerogels [11], which also benefits the recycling of the materials [12,13]. This approach enables the fabrication of cellulose-based adsorbent materials with reduced density and increased volume without compromising their adsorption efficacy. Thus, this strategy effectively addresses the recovery and reuse challenges of adsorbents, leading to more sustainable and efficient water treatment methods.

In environmental remediation, traditional adsorbent materials are effective at capturing contaminants but often lack an integrated indication function for the targeted substances, requiring separate analytical methods to monitor pollutant levels [14,15]. Carbon dots (CDs), a class of nanoscale fluorescent carbon-based materials with diameters <10 nm, offer a novel solution by responding to specific guest molecules, such as mercury ions, through modulated fluorescence emission [16,17]. By encapsulating CDs within a cellulose-based aerogel matrix, the composite material is endowed with photoresponsive characteristics [18,19]. Boron and nitrogen, being C's adjacent elements in the periodic table, can synergistically alter the energy band alignment of CDs. This manipulation results in a distinctive electronic structure that enhances both conductivity and luminescent properties [20]. This innovative design allows for the simultaneous adsorption of mercury ions and the real-time indication of their concentration through fluorescent signals, thereby equipping the adsorbent material with dual functionalities of pollutant capture and in-situ detection [21].

Furthermore, the management of solid waste post-adsorption is a significant environmental challenge, with the potential for exhausted adsorbents to cause secondary pollution [22]. The concept of repurposing spent adsorbents aligns with the principles of a circular economy and promotes sustainable development. In this work, we have successfully synthesized a cellulose-based aerogel encapsulating CDs, which not only exhibits efficient adsorption and removal of mercury and cadmium ions but also demonstrates a highly selective fluorescence response to mercury in aqueous solutions. This dual functionality allows for rapid mercury detection and, after cadmium capture, the material can be treated with Na2S to form a photocatalyst for degrading methylene blue in water, thereby facilitating the material's reuse and further underscoring its utility in environmental remediation. This approach not only mitigates the issue of secondary pollution but also contributes to the development of a more sustainable and eco-friendly waste management strategy, offering significant economic and environmental advantages.

Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO, 6 %–14 %), methylene blue (MB, 70 %), sodium sulfide (Na2S·9H2O, 98 %) and ethylene imine polymer (PEI-600, 99 %) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98 %), urea (99 %), sodium bromide (NaBr, 99.5 %), glutaraldehyde (50 %), citric acid (99.5 %), EDTA-disodium (EDTA-2Na, 99 %) and boric acid (H3BO3, 99.5 %) were obtained from McLean Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. Ethanol, methanol, nitric acid (GR) and tert-butanol were provided by Damao Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Nitric acid was guaranteed reagent and all other liquid reagents were analytical grade and had not been further purified. Additionally, deionized water was made in laboratory.

N, B-CDs was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, 2.0 g (10.4 mmol) of citric acid, 2.0 g (31.6 mmol) of urea and 0.5 g (8.1 mmol) of H3BO3 were added to 40 mL of deionized water, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 15 min. Subsequently, the solution was placed in a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined stainless steel reactor and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. The product was naturally cooled to room temperature and filtrated through 0.22 μm filter membrane. The obtained supernatant was then added into ethanol solution to precipitate a black-green oily liquid. Eventually, N, B-CDs were obtained by desiccation treatment of the oily solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the aerogel adsorbent TOMCC-NH
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic route of the hydrogel adsorbent is illustrated in Scheme 1 . 6.0 g of MCC was dispersed in 100 g of alkali solution of urea (7 wt% NaOH/12 wt% urea) which underwent a freezing (4 h) and subsequent thawing process under continuous stirring to obtain a transparent cellulose solution. Afterwards, the cellulose solution was poured into a specific mold and slowly immersed in methanol coagulation bath to form the hydrogels of the same shape. After curing for 3 h, the hydrogels were soaked in water for 2 days, accompanied by the replacement of water every 6 h to remove alkali and methanol. Then, 5.0 g of cellulose hydrogels were suspended in water with content of 1 wt% and 0.08 g of TEMPO (16 mg/g cellulose) and 0.5 g of NaBr (100 mg/g cellulose) were added into the suspension under vigorous stirring. Subsequently, 7.06 g of NaClO solution was dropwise added to the above mixture of hydrogel and NaOH solution (0.1 M) was used to control the pH of the suspension at a constant value of 10 until all the NaClO solution was depleted. The TEMPO-oxidized hydrogels were then thoroughly washed to neutral with water (η = 78.12 %). After that, the hydrogels were further functionalized by PEI. Specifically, 3.0 g of PEI was added to 100 mL (1 wt%) of the suspension and stirred at room temperature for 2 h. Next, 10 mL of glutaraldehyde solution (2 wt%) was dropwise added and reacted at 60 °C for 4 h. The obtained samples were repeated three times in a mixed solution of ethanol and tert-butanol to remove PEI and washed with deionized water until neutral. Finally, the functionalized hydrogels were immersed in N, B-CDs solution (0.5 mg/mL) for 24 h and the adsorbent material (named as TOMCC-NH) was obtained by freeze-drying technology (η = 74.86 %).

FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet 6700, the United States) was used to analyse the functional group information of the N, B-CDs and adsorbent materials. The SEM observation of samples was performed with a Hitachi S-4800 microscope (Japan). The images of N, B-CDs was photographed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, the United States). BET surface area of obtained samples were measured using N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (ASAP 2420, the United States). In addition, the crystal structure analysis was performed using X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Brooke D8 advantage, Germany). The XPS spectra were measured on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Scientific Nexs, the United States). Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) experiments were performed using FluoroMax Plus (Horiba, the United States). Fluorescence and UV absorption spectra of the samples were recorded by Lengguang Luminescence spectrophotometer (F97PRO, China) and ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (T6 New Century, China), respectively. The compressive strength of the aerogel was measured using an electronic universal testing machine (MTS E43.104, China) with a compression speed of 1.0 mm/min. The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was obtained by a Bruker A300 (Germany). Zeta potentials of the samples were measured with Zetasizer Nano ZS (UK). The swelling rate of the hydrogels was determined by quantitative examination. The swelling ratio of hydrogels was calculated as Eq. (1)
(1)

where W d and W s are the quality of the hydrogels under dry condition and after swelling, respectively. The carboxylate content of the hydrogels was measured by conductometric titration. Briefly, 25 mg of TOMCC-NH was dispersed in 60 mL of NaCl solution (0.83 mmol/L), and the pH was adjusted to 2.5–3 using HCl solution (0.1 M). The mixture was then titrated with NaOH solution (0.1 M). After 1 h of stirring, the pH was regulated to 10 using NaOH solution (0.1 M) and the consumption of NaOH was recorded. The carboxyl content was obtained by Eq. (2):

where C and V are the concentration and volume of the NaOH solution, respectively. m is the quality of TOMCC-NH. In addition, the details of the experiments including fluorimetric analysis, adsorption, regeneration and resource utilization can be obtained in the supporting information section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance and spectral characterization of N, B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image depicted in Fig. 1a illustrates that the N, B-CDs are uniformly dispersed in an aqueous medium, exhibiting a predominantly spherical morphology with a consistent size distribution. The inset of Fig. 1a indicates that the particle size of N, B-CDs ranges from 1 to 7 nm, with an average diameter of approximately 3.53 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image presented in Fig. 1b reveals a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm within the N, B-CDs, which aligns with the (1,0,0) plane of graphite, as referenced in the literature [23]. Further characterization of the N, B-CDs was conducted using FT-IR, with the spectrum provided in Fig. S1. The broad peaks observed at 3439 cm−1 and 3192 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the amino (−NH2) and hydroxyl/nitrogen‑hydrogen (-OH/N-H) groups, respectively. The detection of these functional groups corroborates the successful nitrogen doping, which enhances the hydrophilicity of the N, B-CDs [24]. Fig. S1 also displays additional bands indicative of B–N bonding. Specifically, for sp2-bonded B–N, the absorption peaks at 1350 cm−1 and 780 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of the in-plane B–N bond and the bending vibration of the out-of-plane B-N-B, respectively. For sp3-bonded B–N, the infrared (IR) active transverse optical (TO) mode is observed at 1120 cm−1 [25]. Additionally, the B–O stretching vibration peak appears at 1446 cm−1 and the peak at 1170 cm−1 are attributed to the B–C bond.

To further ascertain the chemical composition of the N, B-CDs, the sample was analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As depicted in Fig. 1c, the survey XPS spectrum confirms that the N, B-CDs consist of carbon (48.71 %), nitrogen (15.52 %), oxygen (30.35 %), and boron (5.42 %), thereby validating the successful incorporation of N and B elements. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1d) exhibits three distinct peaks at 288.79 eV, 286.61 eV, and 284.75 eV, corresponding to -COOH, C-N/C-O, and C-C/C=C groups, respectively. The high-resolution N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1e) further confirms the presence of various nitrogen environments, including N–H (401.54 eV), C–N (399.88 eV), and B–N (398.85 eV) groups. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 1f) have been deconvoluted to reveal three distinct peaks, which correspond to the B–O bond at 533.28 eV, the C-OH group at 532.2 eV, and the C=O group at 531.42 eV, respectively [26]. The B1s binding energy (Fig. 1g) exhibits three peaks at 192.54 eV, 192.05 eV, and 191.63 eV, attributed to the B–O, B–C, and B–N bonds, respectively.

The spectral features of N, B-CDs were further elucidated through UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 1h, the UV absorption spectrum of N, B-CDs exhibits a characteristic π-π* transition around 240 nm and a distinct absorption peak at 340 nm, which is ascribed to the n-π* transition [27]. The N, B-CDs solution appears colorless and transparent under ambient sunlight but displays a vivid blue fluorescence when subjected to ultraviolet illumination, as evidenced by the inset in Fig. 1h. The fluorescence spectrum indicates that at the optimal excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the emission spectrum is dominated by a peak centered at 440 nm. Furthermore, Fig. 1i demonstrates the wavelength-dependent behavior of the N, B-CDs, with the inset revealing a progressive red shift in the emission peak from 436 nm to 448 nm as the excitation wavelength is incrementally increased from 290 nm to 410 nm. This phenomenon is typically attributed to variations in the surface state and particle size of the N, B-CDs [28,29]. To assess the fluorescence stability of the N, B-CDs, the fluorescence intensity was analyzed under varying conditions, including pH fluctuations, solution temperature changes, exposure to sunlight, and salinity variations, as presented in Fig. S2(a-d), indicating that the N, B-CDs exhibit robust fluorescence stability across a range of challenging environmental conditions.

The selection of these parameters reflects a consideration of the typical scenarios that the material may encounter in practical applications. Our findings indicate that the fluorescence of the CDs is greatly quenched at pH levels below 4, aligning with the known acidic sensitivity of most carbon dots. However, the optimal pH range for the utilization of TOMCC-NH adsorbent material lies between 5 and 7. This is attributable to the formation of metal hydroxides in alkaline conditions, which precipitate and greatly diminish the dissolved metal ions. Consequently, within the pH range of 5 to 7, the material's fluorescence emission remains stable (Fig. S2a). In addition, The zeta potential of the CDs within the pH range of 2 to 12 has been examined (Fig. S3). It is revealed that the zeta potential is negative across the entire tested range, suggesting that the material's surface bears a negative charge. The material has relatively robust dispersion stability, particularly within the pH range of 5–6. Regarding the impact of light exposure on the stability of the CDs, we conducted an experiment involving a 36-h period under sunlight irradiation. The fluorescence of the CDs was found to be minimally affected (<4 %) after exposure to sunlight for up to 10 h, and even after 36 h, the fluorescence intensity only decreased by 26 % (Fig. S2b). Furthermore, temperature variations within the range of 10 to 100 °C had a negligible effect on the fluorescence of the CDs (Fig. S2c). Similarly, the ionic strength of the salt solution, when varied from 0 to 2 mg/L, did not significantly influence the fluorescence of the CDs (<6 %) (Fig. S2d).

The functional groups present within the adsorbent were characterized using FTIR to confirm the successful synthesis of the material. The FTIR spectra for MCC, TEMPO-oxidized microcrystalline cellulose (TOMCC), and aminated TOMCC (TOMCC-NH) are displayed in Fig. 2a. Following the TEMPO oxidation process, the emergence of a peak at 1662 cm−1 in the spectrum of TOMCC is indicative of the carbonyl (C=O) stretching vibration associated with the carboxyl group [30]. In contrast to the spectrum of TOMCC, the introduction of new absorption peaks at 3277 cm−1 and 1665 cm−1 in the TOMCC-NH spectrum corresponds to the stretching vibrations of N–H and C=N, respectively, which corroborates the successful fabrication of the adsorbent [31]. Besides, the carboxyl group content in TOMCC-NH is determined to be 0.8 × 10−3 mol/g. In addition, a comparative analysis of the micro-morphological features of MCC and TOMCC-NH (Fig. 2b) further substantiates the successful functionalization of the aerogels. The MCC aerogel is characterized by a dense porous structure arising from hydrogen bond interactions among cellulose molecules. In contrast, the amination treatment with PEI to form TOMCC-NH results in the collapse or obstruction of some pores, a phenomenon potentially attributable to the filling effect of PEI. Concurrently, high magnification SEM images reveal that the internal pore surfaces become flat after amination. Additionally, the elemental mapping of TOMCC (Fig. 2c) discloses the presence of C, O, N, and B elements on the surface of the adsorbent. The content of the four elements is shown in Table S1. The detection of N and B elements proves the successful amination process and the effective doping of N, B-CDs, respectively.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms for TOMCC-NH and MCC gels are depicted in Fig. 2d and Fig. S4, respectively. The isotherm for TOMCC-NH, as delineated in Fig. 2d, exhibits H1-type hysteresis [32]. This is characterized by the presence of a hysteresis loop within the relative pressure range of 0.5 to 1, which is potentially attributed to the condensation within the mesopores of TOMCC-NH, leading to a desorption pressure that exceeds the adsorption pressure [33]. Moreover, as indicated in Table S2, the BET surface area of the material experiences a reduction from 9.5446 m2/g to 4.1251 m2/g subsequent to the amination process. This significant disparity in BET surface area is likely attributed to the alteration in the microstructural characteristics resulting from the filling effect of PEI [34,35]. The incorporation of PEI into the cellulose aerogel leads to the filling of some pores. Furthermore, the modification process also generates the formation of new chemical bonds within the aerogel's pores through chemical crosslinking. This crosslinking diminishes the pore openness and consequently impacts the surface area of the material, a phenomenon consistent with the observations from the SEM images [36]. Concurrently, the introduction of amino groups results in an increase in the gel density from 0.013 g/cm3 to 0.05 g/cm3.

The swelling behavior of TOMCC-NH was investigated, with the kinetic curve presented in Fig. 2e. It is observed that TOMCC-NH rapidly absorbs water within the initial 15 min, achieving swelling equilibrium by 60 min. The swelling ratio reaches a remarkable 1142 %, which is approximately threefold that of the MCC gel. This enhanced swelling is attributed to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups, namely amino and carboxyl groups, on the TOMCC-NH, which significantly augments the hydrophilicity of the hydrogel (Fig. 2f). Such an elevated swelling ratio is anticipated to facilitate the exposure of additional adsorption sites of the adsorbent materials [37].

For the fluorescent sensing materials, the sensitivity and selectivity of the material towards target ions are of paramount importance. The intrinsic optical characteristics of the nanostructured carbon dots (N, B-CDs) are delineated in Fig. 3a, where a pronounced decrease in fluorescence intensity is observed upon the introduction of Hg (II) ions. This response is highly sensitive, with the relative fluorescence quenching efficiency reaching approximately 93 % when the Hg (II) concentration is elevated to 3.5 μM. Furthermore, the relationship between Hg (II) concentration and the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 0/I) of the N, B-CDs solution (where I 0 denotes the initial fluorescence intensity and I represents the intensity in the presence of Hg (II)) is depicted in Fig. 3b. A meticulous examination reveals a flawless linear correlation across two distinct concentration ranges: 0.5–2.0 μM and 2–3.3 μM. The corresponding linear equations are I 0/I = 1.04[Hg2+] + 0.46 for the lower concentration range and I 0/I = 9.42[Hg2+]-17.85 for the higher range. Utilizing the detection limit formula, LOD = 3σ/K, the calculated detection limit for Hg (II) using N, B-CDs is determined to be approximately 1.4 × 10−8 M. Upon attaining concentration of 3.5 μM for a series of metal ions, the fluorescence of CDs is specifically quenched by the presence of Hg (II) ions, as evidenced by Fig. 3c. The high selectivity of N, B-CDs demonstrates the potential of the sensors for mercury ion detection in water. Given that N, B-CDs are embedded within the cellulose gel matrix, their application extends to the fluorescence-based detection and removal of Hg (II) from aqueous environments. To substantiate the reliability of this approach, the fluorescence stability of the TOMCC-NH is assessed by immersing it in deionized water. After a two-week soaking period, negligible leakage of N, B-CDs is observed, as indicated in the inset of Fig. 3d. This stability is attributed to the robust hydrogen bonding interactions between the N, B-CDs and the cellulose gel, ensuring the integrity and effectiveness of the TOMCC-NH material in practical applications. In accordance with the visual representation provided in Fig. 3d, it is observed that the TOMCC-NH exhibits a near-total suppression of blue fluorescence within a brief period of 15 min when immersed in a 3.5 μM Hg (II) solution. Consequently, this time frame has been identified as optimal for conducting concentration-based assays. Subsequent concentration tests reveal a linear attenuation in fluorescence intensity as the Hg (II) concentration escalates from 0.1 to 1 mg/L, thereby underscoring the sensitive nature of TOMCC-NH in detecting and quantifying Hg (II) levels. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S5a, the fluorescence responsiveness of TOMCC-NH was evaluated using instrumental analysis, establishing a detection limit for Hg (II) ions of 2.2 × 10−7 M. Moreover, Fig. S5b in supplementary materials corroborates the selectivity of TOMCC-NH, demonstrating a specific fluorescent response to Hg (II) ions in the absence of any detectable interaction with a series of other metal ions.

The quenching mechanism of Hg (II) ions on N, B-CDs is investigated to understand the principles governing the fluorescence variation. The quenching process is considered to be influenced by the intrinsic composition of the carbon dots and the characteristics of the quenching agents [38]. The surface of N, B-CDs is adorned with various functional groups, which play an important role in the chemical interactions with metal ions and the ensuing fluorescence quenching. Specifically, Hg (II) can coordinate with these surface functional groups, triggering a fluorescence quenching effect. The complexation alters the electronic state of the N, B-CDs, consequently impacting their fluorescence characteristics. The interaction between metal ions and N, B-CDs can facilitate electron transfer from the dots to the metal ions. This electron transfer enhances non-radiative electron-hole recombination, thereby inducing fluorescence quenching. Generally, The common quenching mechanisms encompasses static quenching (SQ), dynamic quenching (DQ), energy transfer processes including fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and Stokes energy transfer (DET), internal filtering effect (IFE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and surface energy transfer (SET) [39]. To ascertain the specific fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of N, B-CDs was measured both in the absence and presence of the Hg (II) ion quencher. As depicted in Fig. 3e, the fluorescence lifetime of N, B-CDs remains invariant upon the introduction of Hg (II), a phenomenon indicative of static quenching. Furthermore, static quenching is typically associated with the formation of a complex between the carbon dots and the quencher, which in turn leads to alterations in the absorption spectra of the carbon dots. Fig. 3f presents a comparative analysis of the absorption spectra between the N, B-CDs in the presence of Hg (II) and the N, B-CDs alone, revealing a marked disparity (with the maximum absorption peaks located at 340 nm and 321 nm, respectively) that suggests the formation of non-emissive ground state complexes [40]. In summary, the collective evidence garnered from these experiments substantiates the occurrence of static quenching as the predominant mechanism responsible for the observed fluorescence deactivation in the presence of Hg (II) ions.

The pH environment plays a pivotal role in influencing both the speciation of metal ions and the surface characteristics of adsorbents, which in turn affects their adsorption efficacy. The influence of pH on the adsorption capacities of Hg (II) and Cd (II) was initially examined across a range of pH values [41]. As delineated in Fig. 4a, the adsorption capacities of TOMCC-NH for Hg (II) and Cd (II) exhibit a pronounced dependence on pH. With the pH of the solution incrementing from 2 to 5, the adsorption capacity for Hg (II) escalates from 44.34 to 182.46 mg/g, while that for Cd (II) rises from 30.25 to 145 mg/g. This trend is attributed to the increased pH values inducing deprotonation of the surface functional groups on TOMCC-NH, culminating in an enhanced negative charge density that subsequently boosts the adsorption of metal ions. Beyond pH 5, the adsorption capacity remains relatively unchanged or exhibits a marginal increase, a behavior that may be ascribed to the precipitation of metal hydroxides. To circumvent the interference of Hg(OH)2 and Cd(OH)2 in the adsorption process, the optimal pH for Hg (II) and Cd (II) adsorption is identified as 5 in the following procedures [42]. Additionally, the point of zero charge (pHpzc) for TOMCC-NH is determined to be approximately 4.9, as depicted in Fig. S6 This indicates that the adsorbent surface bears a positive charge below pH 4.9, while above this pH, it becomes negatively charged. Consequently, the optimal pH for adsorption is established at 5 [43], aligning with the conditions that maximize the electrostatic attraction between the metal ions and the negatively charged adsorbent surface.

The influence of contact time on the adsorption capacities of Hg (II) and Cd (II) in both single and binary systems are investigated at an initial concentration of 200 mg/L. As depicted in Fig. 4b, within the single system, the adsorption capacity of TOMCC-NH for Hg (II) exhibits a rapid increase during the initial phase of adsorption, with the adsorption equilibrium being essentially attained within 90 min. A comparable trend is observed in the binary system, where the adsorption kinetics of Cd (II) parallels that of Hg (II). In essence, both in single and binary systems, the adsorption process is predominantly rapid during the first 40 min, during which the adsorption capacity surpasses 80 % of the equilibrium value. Subsequently, the rate of adsorption decelerates until equilibrium is established. To gain a more profound comprehension of the adsorption dynamics, the adsorption kinetics are fitted using pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order, and intra-particle diffusion models [44,45]. The linear equations corresponding to these three models are articulated in the supplementary material (Text S3.5.2 Adsorption kinetics), providing a quantitative framework to analyse and predict the adsorption behavior of TOMCC-NH towards Hg (II) and Cd (II). The kinetic parameters for both the single and binary adsorption systems have been systematically tabulated in Table S3. The fitting diagrams for the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models are graphically depicted in Supplementary Fig. S7(a-b) and Fig. 4(b-c), respectively. The results reveal that the correlation coefficients R 2 2 for the pseudo-second-order kinetics for both Cd (II) and Hg (II) in the two systems are notably superior to their counterparts R 1 2 in the pseudo-first-order model. Moreover, the equilibrium adsorption capacities derived from the fitting procedures align well with the empirical data, underscoring the predominant chemical adsorption by TOMCC-NH. Fig. 4b and c elucidate that in the binary system, the presence of a competing ion invariably generates a marked reduction in the adsorption capacity, thereby indicating an antagonistic effect in the adsorption of Hg (II) and Cd (II). It is also observed that under identical experimental conditions, Hg (II) seizes the active adsorption sites with greater rapidity and to a larger extent, a phenomenon which may be ascribed to the divergent electronegativities of the two ions. The higher electronegativity of Hg (II) (2.00) as compared to Cd (II) (1.69) renders it more favorably disposed towards effective adsorption on TOMCC-NH.

The intraparticle diffusion mode (Fig. 4d) delineates a three-stage adsorption process. Initially, the adsorption occurs rapidly on the external surface which can be ascribed to the abundance of available adsorption sites. Subsequently, as the surface active sites become depleted, the adsorption process transitions into the diffusion-controlled regime. During the final stage, the rate of adsorption decelerates, culminating in equilibrium. It is pertinent to highlight that the rates of adsorption for these respective stages are in the descending order: k d1 > k d2 > k d3 (Table S4). Furthermore, the four schematic representations of the internal diffusion model exhibit a deviation from the origin, which suggests that the rate-limiting factors in the adsorption process extend beyond mere intraparticle diffusion [44].

Fig. 4e and f illustrate the impact of metal ion concentrations and temperatures on the equilibrium adsorption capacity within a single system. It is found that the equilibrium adsorption capacities for Hg (II) and Cd (II) escalate from 48.45 to 292.3 mg/g and from 39.95 to 211.5 mg/g at 298 K, respectively. This augmentation in adsorption capacity is presumably linked to an enhanced concentration gradient, which facilitates the ability of metal ions to surmount the mass transfer impediments between the liquid and solid phases [46]. The Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models were employed to analyse the data obtained from the single system. The respective model equations are presented in Text S3.5.3 Adsorption isotherms. The adsorption isotherm models and their corresponding fitting data are presented in Fig. 4(e-f), Fig. S8(a-b), and Table S5, respectively.

The Langmuir model more accurately portrays the adsorption behavior of Hg (II) and Cd (II) (R 2 L>R 2 F), and the maximum adsorption capacity (Q m) derived from the Langmuir model aligns closely with the experimental data. This suggests that the surface active sites on TOMCC-NH are uniform, and the adsorption of ions occurs in a monolayer within the single system [47]. Furthermore, Hg (II) exhibits a greater affinity for TOMCC-NH compared to Cd (II), which corroborates the adsorption kinetics. Additionally, a marginal increase in the adsorption capacity for both Hg (II) and Cd (II) is observed upon elevating the temperature from 298 K to 318 K, indicating that higher temperatures are conducive to the adsorption process. This observation further supports the notion that chemical interactions play a predominant role in the adsorption mechanism [48]. In the context of the binary system (Fig. S9), the observed reduction in adsorption capacity may be ascribed to competitive interactions and a subsequent decrease in available binding sites, which is also in agreement with the kinetic findings.

The adsorption thermodynamic parameters, including enthalpy (ΔH, kJ/mol), entropy (ΔS, J/mol·K), and Gibbs free energy (ΔG, kJ/mol) are meticulously investigated to elucidate the underlying thermodynamic nature of the adsorption process. The relevant parameters are calculated using the formulas in Text S3.5.4 Adsorption thermodynamics. The calculated thermodynamic parameters are presented in Table S6. The results indicate that a positive enthalpy change (ΔH > 0) and a negative Gibbs free energy (ΔG < 0) are consistent with the spontaneous endothermic adsorption of metal ions by TOMCC-NH [49].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on TOMCC-NH both prior to and subsequent to the adsorption of metal ions to elucidate the potential adsorption mechanisms. As depicted in Fig. 5a, the emergence of novel peaks at 102.0 eV and 405.1 eV in the XPS spectrum of TOMCC-NH-Hg/Cd corroborates the effective sequestration of Hg (II) and Cd (II) ions. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 5b) exhibits five distinct peaks prior to ion adsorption, situated at 287.70 eV, 286.61 eV, 285.93 eV, 284.80 eV, and 284.22 eV, which are respectively ascribed to the C=O, C–N, C–O, C–C, and C=N functional groups [50]. After adsorption, these peaks are observed to shift to 287.64 eV, 286.18 eV, 285.22 eV, 284.58 eV, and 284.13 eV, with concomitant changes in the content of C–N and C–O (Table S7), which is predominantly attributed to the chelation effect exerted by atoms adjacent to carbon on the metal ions [51]. The N1s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 5c) presents peaks at 400.19 eV and 398.83 eV, which correspond to the -NH- and tertiary amine groups, respectively. Following ion adsorption, the characteristic peaks of -NH- (400.55 eV) and tertiary amine (399.13 eV) are noted to migrate towards higher energy levels. This migration signifies that the nitrogen atoms donate their lone electron pairs to engage in complexation with the metal ions, resulting in a decrement in electron cloud density and a concomitant escalation in binding energy. Concurrently, the reduction in tertiary amine content also confirms the occupation of active sites (Table S8). The high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 5d) delineates peaks at 532.38 eV and 531.60 eV, which are indicative of the C=O and C–O functional groups, respectively. Consistently, upon metal ion adsorption, the binding energy of both the C=O and C–O bonds is observed to undergo a shift to 532.61 eV and 531.90 eV, respectively with the carbonyl content decreasing from 95.01 % to 71.54 % (Table S9), underscoring the pivotal role of oxygen atoms in the adsorption process. Fig. 5e schematically represents the proposed mechanism for the capture of Hg (II) by TOMCC-NH. The XPS data collectively suggest that the adsorption mechanism involves the synergistic interaction of various functional groups present on the TOMCC-NH surface, with the metal ions being sequestered through a combination of chelation and complexation processes.

Herein, a 0.1 M EDTA-2Na solution was employed as the eluent to facilitate the desorption of TOMCC-NH (Fig. 5f). Following five desorption cycles, a marginal reduction in the adsorption efficacy of TOMCC-NH towards Hg (II) and Cd (II) was observed (Table S10). Within the context of a single-metal system, the TOMCC-NH exhibited a remarkable regeneration capacity, with a recovery rate exceeding 75 % post adsorption of the respective metal ions. In a more complex binary-metal system, the regeneration rate was found to be above 65 %, underscoring the robustness of the adsorbent. Nevertheless, in contrast to monadic adsorption systems, the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent for each metal species tends to diminish to a certain extent, indicative of an antagonistic effect. Of particular interest is the observation that the desorption process not only releases the adsorbed metal ions but also restores the fluorescence properties of the adsorbent. Initially, the fluorescence recovery of a pure N, B-CDs solution was investigated. As depicted in Fig. 5g, the fluorescence intensity was notably attenuated upon the introduction of Hg (II). Subsequently, the addition of a 0.1 M EDTA-2Na solution led to a significant restoration of fluorescence, with a recovery rate reaching approximately 87 %. Further investigation into the fluorescence regeneration of TOMCC-NH after desorption revealed that even after four cycles of regeneration, the adsorbent retained its sensitivity to Hg (II), as illustrated in Fig. 5h. These findings suggest that the utilization of EDTA-2Na as a chelating agent for metal ions effectively strips the ions from TOMCC-NH, thereby enabling the reuse of the adsorbent. Moreover, the structural integrity of the adsorbent is preserved following the desorption process, which is a critical factor for its practical application and sustainability. Additionally, the compression stress-strain profile of the TOMCC-NH aerogel is also obtained in Fig. S10. The graph indicates a gradual escalation in stress at strains below 60 %, beyond which the rate of stress increase becomes markedly more pronounced. Owing to the inherent flexibility of cellulose nanofibers, the TOMCC-NH aerogel exhibits remarkable resilience, withstanding compression beyond 80 % strain without undergoing mechanical failure. It achieves a maximum stress threshold of 6.38 MPa, surpassing the values reported for other cellulose-based aerogels in the existing literature [52,53]. This level of compressive stress is instrumental in preserving the structural integrity of materials during practical wastewater treatment processes [54].

To harness the potential of the waste gel (CdS@TOMCC-NH) after adsorption for high-value applications, a photocatalytically active hydrogel, CdS@TOMCC-NH, was synthesized by integrating the exhausted adsorbent with a Na2S solution. The photocatalytic efficacy of the material was assessed using methylene blue (MB) as a representative pollutant. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis (Fig. 6a) revealed distinct diffraction peaks at 2θ = 26.5°, 44.5°, and 51.9°, which correspond to the (111), (220), and (311) crystal planes of CdS, respectively, confirming the successful immobilization of CdS onto the adsorbent matrix. Furthermore, the presence of a peak at approximately 22.7° suggests that the cellulose structure was maintained during the synthesis process [55]. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analyses of CdS@TOMCC-NH are presented in Fig. 6b and c. With the mapping image corresponding to Fig. 6b, the uniform distribution of Cd and S elements across the sample surface is evident, confirming the successful incorporation of CdS. Additionally, the observed spherical stacking structure in Fig. 6c is attributed to the aggregation of CdS particles, further substantiating the successful loading of CdS onto the hydrogel. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted to elucidate the bonding state within the composite. The high-resolution Cd3d spectrum (Fig. 6d) displayed two characteristic peaks at 411.15 eV (Cd 3d3/2) and 404.41 eV (Cd 3d1/2), while the peaks at 161.99 eV and 160.84 eV in the S2p spectrum (Fig. 6e) [56] are attributed to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 of S2− ions in the CdS lattice [57]. Subsequently, a MB degradation experiment was conducted to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of CdS@TOMCC-NH. The visual representation of the catalytic efficacy of CdS@TOMCC-NH towards the degradation of MB is depicted in Fig. 6f, which illustrates the evolution of the visible spectra of the MB solution over varying durations of exposure. A discernible trend is observed where the intensity of the characteristic peak corresponding to MB diminishes progressively as the exposure time increases. Notably, the degradation process culminates in near-total elimination of MB after a period of 150 min, underscoring the superior catalytic capabilities of CdS@TOMCC-NH in facilitating the breakdown of MB. To elucidate the correlation between catalytic activity and time, Fig. 6g presents a graphical plot of the dye concentration ratio (C i/C 0) over time, where C i denotes the concentration of MB at a specific time i, and C 0 represents the initial concentration of MB. Under the illumination of 1 sun, CdS@TOMCC-NH exhibits the most rapid degradation kinetics for MB, achieving a degradation efficiency of 95 % within a mere 90 min. Remarkably, even under ambient natural light conditions, the catalytic system maintains a high efficiency, surpassing 93 % within a 240 min timeframe. In addition, Table S11 presents a comparison between TOMCC-NH and other adsorbent materials, showing this sorbent is distinguished by its simplicity, eco-friendliness, and cost-effectiveness, along with its potential for multifaceted applications.

Conversely, in the absence of light, the catalytic degradation of MB by CdS@TOMCC-NH is negligible, thus confirming that the degradation process is primarily driven by light exposure. This observation is further substantiated by the analysis of the degradation mechanism, as delineated in Fig. 6h. The presence of scavengers diminishes the degradation efficacy of the material towards MB, implying that key reactive species such as photogenerated holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH), and superoxide radical (⋅O2 −) are implicated in the degradation process, with the superoxide radical (⋅O2 −) emerging as the most critical mediator in the photocatalytic degradation. To substantiate the presence of active species in the degradation process, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was also employed (Fig. S11). Under illumination, distinct spectral features are observed that are not present in the complete absence of light. Specifically, the six characteristic peaks corresponding to the DMPO-·O2 − adduct are evident (Fig. S11a), along with a pronounced EPR signal attributable to the DMPO-·OH adduct (Fig. S11b). Furthermore, the TEMPO signal was markedly diminished upon exposure to light (Fig. S11c), which confirms the generation of ·OH, ·O2 −, and h+ during the photocatalytic degradation process [58]. The hypothesized mechanism is described through a series of Eqs. (3)–(8):

Initially, CdS absorbs photons, thereby initiating the formation of photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) as depicted in Eq. (3). Following this, the adsorbed oxygen (O2) on the catalyst's surface undergoes reduction by the photogenerated electrons to produce ⋅O2 − (Eq. (4)). Subsequently, the superoxide radicals react with H2O to generate hydroxyl (OH) and perhydroxyl (HO2 −) radicals (Eq. (5)). Concurrently, the holes (h+) interact with the generated OH to form highly reactive hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) (Eq. (6)). The perhydroxyl radicals, in conjunction with surface water, facilitate the degradation of MB, as illustrated in Eqs. (7)–(8) [59,60].

To assess the stability and reusability of the CdS@TOMCC-NH composite, a suspension containing 0.05 g of the material in 100 mL of deionized water was exposed to simulated sunlight (1 sun) for duration of 300 min. After exposure, the composite was filtered, and MB was introduced to the filtrate to achieve a concentration of 15 mg/L. The absorbance of the solution was then monitored. The negligible change in absorbance confirms the stable immobilization of CdS onto the TOMCC-NH matrix. Furthermore, the recycling potential of CdS@TOMCC-NH for MB degradation was evaluated through six consecutive degradation experiments. As depicted in Fig. 6i, even after six cycles of reuse, the catalyst maintained a degradation efficiency exceeding 90 %. This result underscores the robust catalytic activity and operational stability of the material, making it a promising candidate for sustainable environmental applications.

In summary, a three-dimensional porous material (TOMCC-NH), encapsulated with N, B-CDs was successfully synthesized for the dual purpose of adsorbing and detecting mercury ions. The material's exceptional hydrophilicity, evidenced by a swelling rate of 1142 %, renders it highly suitable for aqueous applications. TOMCC-NH demonstrates substantial adsorption capacities for both Hg (II) and Cd (II), with values of 319.49 mg/g and 263.16 mg/g, respectively. Furthermore, the material is capable of detecting mercury ions through a fluorescence response with a detection limit as low as 2.2 × 10−7 M. Additionally, TOMCC-NH exhibits commendable regeneration properties, retaining up to 75 % of its initial adsorption capacity after five consecutive cycles. Notably, the adsorbent material, following the adsorption of Cd (II), could be subjected to in-situ sulfofication and is subsequently utilized for the degradation of organic pollutants (MB) with an impressive degradation efficiency of 95 %. The successful implementation of this strategy not only offers a promising approach for the treatment of heavy metal-polluted wastewater but also presents a viable recycling strategy for the valuable materials from waste adsorbents.
